judgement_by: Hon. D. F. H. Gunawardhana, J.
keywords: The original grantee died and his wife had not succeeded within six months of his death; she had sold her rights to a third party. In the meantime, the 4 th Respondent, the next of kin in the line of succession (the original grantee’s eldest son), was convicted for a triple murder which was later affirmed by the Supreme Court. Therefore, certain functionaries under the Land Development Ordinance, in the belief that he had lost his civil rights, pending the execution of his capital punishment imposed upon him, had decided to accept his younger brother as the next of kin and successor, and accordingly took steps to register his name erroneously. Thereafter, he has transferred his rights to the present Petitioner. The 4th Respondent made an application to succeed. On the advice of the Attorney General, the Commissioner General of Lands directed the 3rd Respondent (Divisional Secretary) to accept the 4th Respondent as the successor; the Petitioner challenged the same. Held – Application dismissed; the Attorney General’s advice is the correct; at certain times, certain functionaries under the Land Development Ordinance, having misread and misinterpreted the legal provisions, had misapplied them.
legistation: The Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka

judgement_by: Hon. Dr. D. F. H. Gunawardhana, J.
keywords: The Petitioner claims that his uncle (3rd Respondent) failed to succeed to the rights as a successor of his late grandfather, who had been the original grantee of a parcel of land given under the Land Development Ordinance. However, the Respondents’ stand is that the 3rd Respondent could not have succeeded after the death of the original grantee in 1996 until the death of the spouse of the original grantee (mother of the 3rd Respondent) in terms of Section 48B. The question of succession arose thereafter in 2022, and the 3rd Respondent has duly applied to register his succession which had been nominated by the original grantee under the original permit issued prior to the grant. Held – acceptance of the 3rd Respondent as the successor by the Divisional Secretary on the instructions of the Commissioner General of Land is valid, and therefore, the registration in the Land Registry of the 3rd Respondent as the successor to the original grantee is valid; Application dismissed.
legistation: The Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka

parties: Withanage Kamani Perera Vs. State Mortgage and Investment Bank and Others Withanage Kamani Perera Vs. State Mortgage and Investment Bank and Others View More
judgement_by: Hon. Dr. D. F. H. Gunawardhana, J.
keywords: The Petitioner was an Attorney-at-Law attached to the legal division of the 1st Respondent Bank; was unduly and irrationally denied third extension after completing 57 years of service, which was challenged successfully; according to the judgement, she was reinstated from the date of the judgement. After reinstatement and service of two more years, at retirement she asked for the back- wages and other emoluments, which was denied; challenged the same. Respondents took up the position that there was a break in service; therefore, not entitled to back-wages during which she had not worked until she was reinstated. Held – break of her service was not caused by the Petitioner but by the Respondents; therefore, entitled to the back-wages and other emoluments.
legistation: The Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka

description: Kohombanghe Buddhika Sampath Kumara Vs The Principal of Richmond College, Galle and Others Kohombanghe Buddhika Sampath Kumara Vs The Principal of Richmond College, Galle and Others View More before Hon. . D. F. H. Gunawardhana, J.
parties: Kohombanghe Buddhika Sampath Kumara Vs The Principal of Richmond College, Galle and Others Kohombanghe Buddhika Sampath Kumara Vs The Principal of Richmond College, Galle and Others View More
judgement_by: Hon. . D. F. H. Gunawardhana, J.
keywords: The Petitioner being the father of the child sought the admission of his child to Grade One at Richmond College, Galle, on the basis of Chief Occupant (CO) and Religious categories, being a non-Roman Catholic Christian; application was refused on the basis that he could not establish CO category based on a valid deed or any other document. However, Petitioner had been allocated a parcel of land by the Land Reform Commission (LRC), pending its transfer in favour of the Petitioner; to which effect the Petitioner had produced documents issued by the LRC addressed to the Principal, in addition to other supporting documents. Held – The Petitioner has sufficiently proved that he falls within the CO category to gain admission.
legistation: The Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka

keywords: Two Petitioners who went on surveillance patrol on the directions given by the OIC, arrested a person with 400 grams of cannabis; after causing him to be produced before the Magistrate on a B-report signed by the OIC, later alleged that the two Petitioners having seized and arrested two accused with 10kg of cannabis, had released one with 9kg and 600g of cannabis on accepting a bribe. After inquiry, their services were terminated, with appeals refused by PSC and AAT; the same was challenged, and Court held that they were convicted without any sufficient evidence, and therefore, writs lie.

parties: The Anuradhapura Vivekananda Society Limited Petitioner Vs. The Divisional Secretary of Nuwaragam Palatha East Anuradhapura and Others The Anuradhapura Vivekananda Society Limited Petitioner Vs. The Divisional Secretary of Nuwaragam Palatha East Anuradhapura and Others View More
judgement_by: Hon. Dr. D. F. H. Gunawardhana, J.
keywords: The Petitioner, a Society running a religious institute and Hindu Temple, was occupying a land within the vicinity of the Sri Maha Bodhi in the sacred city of Anuradhapura. Later, with the dawn of the Buddha Jayanthi era of 1956, the Petitioner was relocated by giving a land, where the Petitioner also ran a school which was vested in the Government under the Assisted Schools and Training Colleges Act. Thereafter, the Petitioner was placed in possession of another land, where it operated a religious institute including a Temple, cultural centre, and restaurant. The excess land was blocked out and given to the 8th to 10th Respondents. Dispute arose between them with regard to the regularisation and legalisation of their possession by the Government on the recommendation of the Provincial Land Commissioner, which the Petitioner objected to. Held - Application dismissed on the basis that the Petitioner has no legal title to claim nor any legitimate expectation to claim any title document since the Petitioner has failed to improve or develop the entire land; the Petitioner is not entitled to benefit out of his own fault.
legistation: The Constitution of the Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka
